body_txt: Under Hollywood History , all historical Pre-Columbian Civilizations (ancient Mexican , Central, and South American nations, before their conquests by the Spanish ) have been portrayed across many different media. The degree of factual accuracy has been all over the place, resulting in a wide array of common traits which either exaggerate, oversimplify, or otherwise misrepresent or misunderstand the cultures and customs of Mesoamerican civilizations. This trope typically comes in two flavors. Some works only portray one particular pre-Columbian civilization, but alter some or most of the historical facts, and therefore present deviations in their customs, culture or even geography; the work may even confuse details from this civilization with those of a non-Mesoamerican culture (like Ancient Egypt or even Medieval Europe). In other works, we might see portrayals which lump or conflate traits from two or more pre-Columbian societies, thus featuring aspects of the Maya (in modern Yucatan peninsula and Central America), Inca (in modern Peru ) and Aztec (in modern Central Mexico), plus many others (especially the Olmec, one of the oldest, as more continues to be discovered about them); this makes up for a tossed salad of exciting bits from all their histories, with a topping of myth and fiction. Indeed, the trope's name is a portmanteau of May a, Inca , and Az tec , but its scope also includes cases of Hollywood History portrayals of individual civilizations. At any rate, expect the inhabitants to be presented as barbaric, or at least distrustful towards whatever foreigners dare to arrive their lands. And regardless of context, fiction will emphasize the exotic nature of the setting: Dressing is powered by blood , aliens are involved, The End of the World as We Know It is imminent, and so on. In more fantastical stories, you can expect these people to be scantily clad, and often a type of Lizard Folk . Perhaps they might even have a City of Gold in there somewhere. Generally, the Mayincatec are more likely to be the villains than the heroes, and as such they are prone to Historical Villain Upgrade . An exception occurs if the story involves the Conquistadores , in which case they'll instead be portrayed as tragic victims of European expansion. Some Alternate History stories have them survive to the modern day, resulting in a Modern Mayincatec Empire . In Real Life , the Maya, the Inca, and the Aztec (which is actually a 18th century exonym; the people called themselves Mexica) were all distinct Pre-Columbian cultures. The Maya and Aztec were comparatively close together, as they both lived in what is now Mexico, and did engage in enough cultural exchange to share many cultural aspects, but this is no reason to conflate them. Meanwhile, the Inca were thousands of miles away from them, their capital being in today's Peru, so conflating them makes even less sense; their actual history is interesting and diverges from the trope quite a bit. There seems to have been some long-distance contact between the cultures (at a minimum, maize had been introduced to the Andes from Mesoamerica), but it was tenuous enough that the Mesoamerican and Andean civilizations were only vaguely aware of each other at best. Ironically, this trope only became more Truth in Television after the Spanish Conquest of America, as the political unification of lands from modern Mexico to modern Chile only intensified cultural exchange. For instance, Náhuatl, the language original to the peoples of Central Mexico, is spoken today in remote locations where few to no Nahua people ever lived, as a consequence of the Spanish Empire adopting it as an official language in order to facilitate administration and evangelization. The founding of new cities, free from the previous political differences, also led to various indigenous groups resettling to the same population centers and suddenly becoming neighbors (sometimes after having been enemies up to that point ). The presence of native artists, translators and scholars in the imperial expansion also mixed things even more, with native cultures intentional or unintentionally influencing each other before meeting the western filter. You can check an extensive list of specific stereotypes and traits in the Analysis subpage , including explanations regarding their accuracy or dramatization. Further information debunking the inaccuracies can be found in the following articles: Pre-Columbian Civilizations , Native American Mythology , Aztec Mythology , Inca Mythology , and Mayan Mythology . See Also: Hollywood History and Very Loosely Based on a True Story . Compare Spexico , Latin Land , The Capital of Brazil Is Buenos Aires and Banana Republic , for when this happens to modern Latin American countries. Also compare Injun Country and Tipis and Totem Poles for composite versions of Native American cultures from North America. European equivalents include Ancient Grome , when Roman and Greek cultures are mashed up together in Ancient Rome and Ancient Greece settings; Scotireland , where distinctly Scottish and Irish elements are mixed up indiscriminately; and Norse by Norsewest , where elements of the Nordic countries are combined together. An African equivalent would be Africa Is a Country . See Egypt Is Still Ancient , which is when you have Egypt depicted as if it were still a land of Pharaohs and gods.
